question_text: Provision for tax made during the year is shown in the Statement of Profit and Loss as:
explanation: Provision for tax (current tax) is shown as 'Tax Expense' in the Statement of Profit and Loss after Profit Before Tax. It represents the estimated income tax liability for the current period based on taxable income.
subject: accountancy

options.text: As Tax Expense after Profit Before Tax

elimination_hints.hint: Tax is not an operating expense — it has its own specific placement after PBT.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating tax as a regular expense.

elimination_hints.hint: Tax is never deducted from revenue. It is computed on profit.
Finance Costs relate to borrowing costs, not income tax.

question_text: Proposed dividend for the current year, as per the Companies Act, 2013:
explanation: After the amendment in AS 4 and as per the Companies Act, 2013, proposed dividend is NOT shown as a provision or liability in the Balance Sheet. It is only disclosed as a Note to the financial statements. It becomes a liability only when declared/approved by shareholders at the AGM.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Is shown as a current liability in the Balance Sheet
Is deducted from Surplus in the Balance Sheet
Is disclosed only as a Note to the financial statements
Is shown under Provisions in Non-Current Liabilities

elimination_hints.hint: Post-2013, proposed dividend is not a liability until declared at AGM.
Only declared/interim dividend is deducted from surplus, not proposed dividend.
Provisions are for probable outflows — proposed dividend is contingent on shareholder approval.

question_text: Interim dividend paid during the year is:
explanation: Interim dividend, once paid/declared by the Board of Directors, is treated as an appropriation of profits. It is deducted from 'Surplus' (i.e., Statement of Profit and Loss balance) under Reserves and Surplus in the Balance Sheet. It is not an expense in the P&L.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Deducted from Surplus in Reserves and Surplus
Shown as an expense in the Statement of Profit and Loss
Only disclosed in Notes, not recorded

elimination_hints.hint: Dividend is an appropriation of profit, not a charge against profit.
Once paid, it reduces surplus — it is not a liability.
Interim dividend actually paid must be recorded, unlike proposed dividend.

question_text: Statement: Transfer to General Reserve from profits is shown as an expense in the Statement of Profit and Loss.
explanation: This is FALSE. Transfer to General Reserve is an appropriation of profit, NOT an expense. It is shown in the Notes to Accounts under Reserves and Surplus. The surplus (P&L balance) is reduced by the amount transferred to General Reserve.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Reserve transfer is an appropriation (distribution of profit), not a charge against profit.

question_text: A company has Surplus (P&L balance) brought forward ₹5,00,000. Net Profit for the year is ₹12,00,000. Transfer to General Reserve is ₹2,00,000 and Interim Dividend paid is ₹3,00,000. What is the closing balance of Surplus?
explanation: Closing Surplus = Opening Surplus + Net Profit – Transfer to General Reserve – Interim Dividend = ₹5,00,000 + ₹12,00,000 – ₹2,00,000 – ₹3,00,000 = ₹12,00,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: You may have forgotten to add the opening surplus of ₹5,00,000.
Check if you deducted both General Reserve transfer and Interim Dividend.
Did you forget to deduct the Interim Dividend of ₹3,00,000?

question_text: Depreciation charged during the year but not yet recorded requires which adjustment?
explanation: Unrecorded depreciation needs two adjustments: (1) Debit Depreciation and Amortisation Expense in the Statement of P&L (increases expenses, reduces profit), and (2) Deduct from the concerned fixed asset in the Balance Sheet (or add to accumulated depreciation). This reduces both profit and asset value.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Add to expenses in P&L only
Add to expenses in P&L and deduct from asset in Balance Sheet
Deduct from asset in Balance Sheet only
Show as a contingent liability in Notes

elimination_hints.hint: Depreciation has a dual effect — it affects both the P&L and the Balance Sheet.
The expense must also be recognised in the P&L to match costs with revenues.
Depreciation is a definite expense, not a contingent liability.

question_text: Outstanding expenses at year-end are shown in the Balance Sheet as:
explanation: Outstanding expenses represent expenses incurred but not yet paid. They are obligations that need to be settled, hence shown as 'Other Current Liabilities' in the Balance Sheet. They are also added to the respective expense in the Statement of P&L.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Outstanding expenses are typically payable within the current period, making them current liabilities.
Provisions involve uncertain timing or amount. Outstanding expenses have known amounts.
Reserves and Surplus is part of Shareholders' Funds, not liabilities.

question_text: Prepaid insurance at year-end is classified in the Balance Sheet under:
explanation: Prepaid insurance represents advance payment for future periods. It is an asset (benefit yet to be received) classified as 'Short-term Loans and Advances' or 'Other Current Assets' under Current Assets in the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Short-term Loans and Advances / Other Current Assets

elimination_hints.hint: Prepaid insurance is typically for the next year — a short-term asset, not non-current.
Prepaid expenses are assets, not liabilities — the company has paid in advance.
Reserves and Surplus represent accumulated profits, not prepayments.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Accrued income (income earned but not received) is shown under 'Other Current Assets' in the Balance Sheet.
Reason (R): Accrued income represents a right to receive payment, making it an asset of the company.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Accrued income (e.g., interest earned but not received) creates a right to receive payment, which is an asset. Since it is typically receivable within the operating cycle, it is classified under Current Assets as 'Other Current Assets'.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY accrued income is an asset — because it represents a receivable right.
R is correct — accrued income does create an enforceable right to receive payment.
A is correct — accrued income IS shown under Other Current Assets.

question_text: A company has machinery worth ₹20,00,000. Depreciation for the year (not yet recorded) is ₹2,00,000. After adjustment, machinery will appear in the Balance Sheet at:
explanation: Machinery is shown at cost less accumulated depreciation. After recording depreciation of ₹2,00,000, the machinery will appear at ₹20,00,000 – ₹2,00,000 = ₹18,00,000. The depreciation amount goes to the P&L as an expense.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹20,00,000 is the gross value before depreciation — the question asks for after adjustment.
Depreciation reduces the asset value, not increases it.
₹2,00,000 is only the depreciation amount, not the net book value of machinery.

question_text: Interest on debentures due but not paid at year-end requires which treatment?
explanation: Interest on debentures due but not paid must be: (1) Added to Finance Costs in the Statement of P&L, and (2) Shown as 'Interest Accrued but Not Due on Borrowings' or 'Other Current Liabilities' in the Balance Sheet. This ensures both expense recognition and liability recording.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Show only in Notes to Accounts
Add to Finance Costs in P&L only
Add to Finance Costs in P&L and show as Current Liability in Balance Sheet

elimination_hints.hint: Accrued interest must be recorded in the books, not just disclosed in Notes.
Interest due does not reduce the principal of debentures — it is a separate liability.
Dual effect — if P&L shows the expense, the corresponding liability must appear in the Balance Sheet.

question_text: Notes to Accounts form an ______ part of the financial statements as per Schedule III.
explanation: Schedule III states that Notes to Accounts form an 'integral' part of the financial statements. They provide essential supplementary information, detailed breakups, accounting policies, and additional disclosures required for a true and fair view.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Notes are mandatory, not optional — Schedule III requires them.
'Supplementary' suggests additional but not essential. Notes are 'integral' — essential and inseparable.
Notes are part of financial statements, not independent of them.

question_text: Which of the following must be disclosed in Notes to Accounts?
explanation: Notes to Accounts must disclose accounting policies adopted by the company. This is a mandatory requirement under Schedule III and AS-1 (Disclosure of Accounting Policies). Key policies include depreciation method, inventory valuation, revenue recognition, etc.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Only specific employee disclosures (like managerial remuneration) are required, not all employee names.
Financial statements are historical; future plans are part of management discussion, not Notes.
Market share is competitive intelligence, not a financial statement disclosure.

question_text: If a company has an unrecorded liability for outstanding salary ₹50,000 and unrecorded income receivable ₹30,000 at year-end, what is the net effect on Profit Before Tax?
explanation: Outstanding salary ₹50,000 increases expenses (reduces profit by ₹50,000). Income receivable ₹30,000 increases income (increases profit by ₹30,000). Net effect = –₹50,000 + ₹30,000 = –₹20,000. Profit Before Tax decreases by ₹20,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Outstanding salary has a LARGER negative effect (₹50,000) than income receivable's positive effect (₹30,000).
₹80,000 would be the sum, but these adjustments have opposite effects — one reduces and one increases profit.
Same error — adding both amounts. The effects are opposite.

question_text: Statement: Provision for doubtful debts is created by debiting the Statement of Profit and Loss and crediting the Trade Receivables directly.
explanation: This is FALSE. The provision for doubtful debts is debited to the Statement of Profit and Loss (as an expense under 'Other Expenses'), but it is NOT credited directly to Trade Receivables. Instead, a separate 'Provision for Doubtful Debts' account is created and shown as a deduction from Trade Receivables in the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: While P&L is debited, the credit goes to a separate Provision account, NOT directly to Trade Receivables.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing bad debts written off with provision.

question_text: A company has Trade Receivables of ₹10,00,000. Provision for Doubtful Debts to be maintained at 5%. Existing provision is ₹30,000. What additional provision is charged to P&L?
explanation: Required provision = 5% of ₹10,00,000 = ₹50,000. Existing provision = ₹30,000. Additional provision needed = ₹50,000 – ₹30,000 = ₹20,000. Only the additional ₹20,000 is charged to the Statement of P&L as an expense.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50,000 is the total required provision. But ₹30,000 already exists, so only the shortfall is charged.
₹30,000 is the existing provision, not the additional charge required.
₹80,000 incorrectly adds existing and required provisions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding instead of finding the difference.

question_text: Income received in advance at year-end is shown in the Balance Sheet as:
explanation: Income received in advance (unearned revenue) represents money received for services/goods not yet delivered. It is an obligation to perform in the future, hence classified as a Current Liability under 'Other Current Liabilities'.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: It is received but not earned — the company owes a service, making it a liability.
Reserves represent accumulated profits, not advance receipts.
Only earned revenue is shown in P&L. Advance income is deferred until earned.

question_text:
Match the adjustment items with their Balance Sheet classification:

Column I:
(i) Prepaid rent
(ii) Outstanding wages
(iii) Provision for tax
(iv) Accrued interest receivable

Column II:
(P) Short-term Provisions
(Q) Other Current Liabilities
(R) Other Current Assets
(S) Short-term Loans and Advances
explanation: Prepaid rent is an advance payment — shown under Short-term Loans and Advances (S). Outstanding wages are unpaid expenses — Other Current Liabilities (Q). Provision for tax is a short-term provision (P). Accrued interest receivable is income earned but not received — Other Current Assets (R).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Prepaid rent is an advance payment (Loans & Advances), not Other Current Assets. Accrued interest is Other Current Assets.
This option has multiple mismatches — wages are liabilities (Q), not advances (S).
Outstanding wages (ii) are unpaid obligations = Other Current Liabilities (Q), not Provisions (P). Tax provision is correctly (P).

question_text: Contingent liabilities are:
explanation: Contingent liabilities are possible obligations that arise from past events and whose existence will be confirmed by uncertain future events. As per Schedule III and AS 29, they are NOT recorded in the Balance Sheet but are disclosed by way of a Note to the financial statements. Examples include pending lawsuits and guarantees given.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Shown as Current Liabilities in the Balance Sheet
Shown as Non-Current Liabilities in the Balance Sheet
Recorded as Provisions in the Balance Sheet
Disclosed by way of Notes to the financial statements only

elimination_hints.hint: Contingent liabilities are uncertain — they are not recorded in the Balance Sheet at all.
Same as above — they are disclosed in Notes, not recorded as liabilities.
Provisions are for probable obligations with estimated amounts. Contingent liabilities are possible, not probable.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Goodwill appearing in the Balance Sheet of a company must be shown under 'Intangible Assets' in Non-Current Assets.
Reason (R): Goodwill is a non-physical asset that provides long-term benefits to the company.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A. Goodwill is an intangible (non-physical) asset that represents future economic benefits arising from assets not individually identified. As per Schedule III, it is shown under 'Intangible Assets' within Non-Current Assets because it provides benefits over multiple periods.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY goodwill is under intangible non-current assets — because it is non-physical and long-term.
R is true — goodwill IS a non-physical asset providing long-term economic benefits.
A is true — Schedule III explicitly places goodwill under Intangible Assets in Non-Current Assets.
